attributes.stem:
A 76-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a two-hour history of sudden onset abdominal pain, rated 9/10 in severity. The pain is constant and widespread. Her past medical history includes hypertension and heart failure. She has no history of gallstone disease. Her medications include ramipril and bisoprolol. She does not drink alcohol and has never smoked.

On examination, her blood pressure is 115/70 mmHg, pulse is 100/min and irregular, and temperature is 37.2°C. There is widespread abdominal tenderness, most severe in the periumbilical region.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following blood tests will be most useful in diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The stem describes a patient with rapid onset of severe generalised abdominal pain. This is very likely to be an ischaemic event and with her cardiovascular history this may well be acute mesenteric ischaemia as a complication of undiagnosed atrial fibrillation. Elevated serum lactate is a hallmark of ischaemia and would support this diagnosis. Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) is also likely to be elevated, however this is less specific for acute ischaemia. LDH is released from damaged tissue of any aetiology: ischaemic, infective or inflammatory.

Full blood count may reveal leucocytosis and C-reactive protein may be elevated but these are both nonspecific features of inflammation.

Elevated serum lipase would be suggestive of pancreatic damage. This is an important test to order when investigating the acute abdomen. However, the pain associated with acute pancreatitis is usually more gradual in onset and confined to the upper abdomen. However, in this case, serum lipase is not the priority. It would not be useful in ruling in the most likely differential - only ruling out acute pancreatitis. Acute pancreatitis would be classically present with epigastric pain (+/- radiation to the back).

The confirmatory imaging for mesenteric ischaemia is CT angiogram, which will identify the location of the vascular occlusion and guide the surgical approach.
learningPoints: Serum lactate is a marker for ischaemia.
Acute mesenteric ischaemia should be suspected in elderly patients with sudden severe abdominal pain and cardiovascular risk factors.
references: Bala M, Catena F, Kashuk J, De Simone B, Gomes CA, Weber D, Sartelli M, Coccolini F, Kluger Y, Abu-Zidan FM, Picetti E, Ansaloni L, Augustin G, Biffl WL, Ceresoli M, Chiara O, Chiarugi M, Coimbra R, Cui Y, Damaskos D, Di Saverio S, Galante JM, Khokha V, Kirkpatrick AW, Inaba K, Leppäniemi A, Litvin A, Peitzman AB, Shelat VG, Sugrue M, Tolonen M, Rizoli S, Sall I, Beka SG, Di Carlo I, Ten Broek R, Mircea C, Tebala G, Pisano M, van Goor H, Maier RV, Jeekel H, Civil I, Hecker A, Tan E, Soreide K, Lee MJ, Wani I, Bonavina L, Malangoni MA, Koike K, Velmahos GC, Fraga GP, Fette A, de'Angelis N, Balogh ZJ, Scalea TM, Sganga G, Kelly MD, Khan J, Stahel PF, Moore EE. Acute mesenteric ischemia: updated guidelines of the World Society of Emergency Surgery. World J Emerg Surg. 2022 Oct 19;17(1):54. doi: 10.1186/s13017-022-00443-x.
Isfordink CJ, Dekker D, Monkelbaan JF. Clinical value of serum lactate measurement in diagnosing acute mesenteric ischaemia. Neth J Med. 2018 Mar;76(2):60-64. PMID: 29515007.

Kintu-Luwaga R, Galukande M, Owori FN. Serum lactate and phosphate as biomarkers of intestinal ischemia in a Ugandan tertiary hospital: a cross-sectional study. Int J Emerg Med. 2013 Dec 4;6(1):44. doi: 10.1186/1865-1380-6-44. PMID: 24304560; PMCID: PMC4177190.
